identified_property: severity of debt terms and creditor

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: You owe $1,000 to a violent loan shark who charges 50% weekly compounding interest and has threatened serious harm if you don't repay in full by the end of the week.

variations.label: low
variations.text: You owe $1,000 to an aggressive collections agency charging 30% annual interest, with the power to garnish your wages and seize your assets if you miss a single payment.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: You owe $1,000 to a bank on a high-interest credit line at 25% APR, with late fees accumulating and your credit score dropping each month you carry the balance.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: You owe $1,000 to a patient acquaintance who has said there's no interest and asked you to pay it back whenever you're able over the next year.

variations.label: high
variations.text: You owe $1,000 to a close friend who insists there's no interest, no deadline, and has told you not to stress about it at all.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: You owe $1,000 to a wealthy family member who charges no interest, has set no repayment deadline, and has strongly hinted they'd be happy to forgive the debt entirely.

quality_checks.reasoning: All tiers describe the same core scenario—owing $1,000—but vary the creditor identity (loan shark → collections agency → bank → unspecified → acquaintance → friend → family) and debt terms (violent threats with extreme interest → wage garnishment → high APR with credit damage → neutral → no interest with flexible timeline → no pressure → likely forgiveness). Each step meaningfully reduces severity.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because owing $1,000 to a forgiving wealthy family member with no interest, no deadline, and a strong likelihood of debt forgiveness is the mildest possible version of this debt scenario, minimizing financial stress, personal risk, and relational strain.
